facts: The patient's name is Wayne.
The patient has a sore on his foot.
The sore is not healing.
The patient has had the wound for a couple of weeks.
The patient saw his primary care physician (PCP) about the wound.
The PCP referred the patient to the doctor.
The patient used to see a podiatrist.
The patient has not seen a podiatrist for a couple of years.
The patient had a blister on his foot.
The blister was caused by a new pair of shoes.
The patient has neuropathy.
The patient did not feel that the shoes were too tight at first.
The patient has been putting a band-aid on the wound.
The patient finished a course of antibiotics for the wound.
The patient finished the antibiotics the day before the consultation.
The wound looked the same after the antibiotics.
The patient does not feel pain in the wound.
The patient has no fever.
The patient has no chills.
The patient has no drainage from the wound.
The wound sometimes sticks to the patient's sock.
The patient is no longer wearing the shoes that caused the problem.
The shoes that caused the problem are Hoka's.
The patient got the shoes at REI.
The patient likes the shoes because they are comfortable.
The patient likes the shoes because they are easy to take on and off.
The patient likes the shoes because they provide good stability.
The patient has had issues with stability.
The patient has diabetes.
The patient's last HbA1c was a little over 8.
The doctor performed a physical exam.
The patient's temperature is 98.1.
The patient's heart rate is 72.
The patient's respirations are 16.
The patient's blood pressure is 110 over 65.
The patient has a 1 by 2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot.
The wound is just proximal to the right fifth metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint.
The wound has some yellow slough present.
The wound has minimal granulation tissue.
The wound has no surrounding erythema or cellulitis.
The wound has no evidence of fluid collection.
The wound has no necrosis.
The wound has no odor.
The patient has no bony exposure.
The patient has palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses.
The patient has no palpable dorsal pedis (DP) or posterior tibial (PT) pulses.
The patient has Doppler signs present.
The doctor did not appreciate any pain when touching the wound.
The patient's right foot x-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
The doctor is going to order an ankle-brachial index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the patient's foot.
The doctor is going to perform a debridement to take off some of the dead tissue.
The doctor prescribed collagenase ointment.
Collagenase ointment is to be applied to the wound once daily.
The collagenase ointment is for the wound.
The patient is to cover the wound with a dry sterile dressing.
The patient is to wear a surgical shoe to take pressure off the area.
The doctor does not think any more antibiotics are needed at this time.
The doctor wants to see the patient again in two weeks.
The doctor may refer the patient to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates the blood supply is not optimal for wound healing.
The doctor wants to get the patient's diabetes under control to help the wound heal.
The doctor may get a diabetic consult to help the patient modify his diet.
The doctor wants to bring the patient's hemoglobin A1c into a better level.
The doctor prescribed collagenase ointment to the patient.
The collagenase ointment dose is not specified.
The collagenase ointment frequency is once daily.
The collagenase ointment is for the diabetic foot ulcer.
The follow-up appointment is in two weeks.
